coredata.dc:title: Printing guidelines for extrusion-based additive manufacturing with potential use in lithium-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yangyang
Gao, Binghuan
Park, Sujin
Jang, Seongwan
Son, Hyeonjin
Bae, Chang-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Extrusion-based additive manufacturing significantly affects the performance of lithium-ion battery through structural heterogeneities, while there are no printing guidelines able to predict accuracy of printed features. Herein, an empirical model for geometric prediction is proposed and verified to fabricate 3D battery based on fundamental understanding of ink rheology and printing behavior. Lower viscosity of ink rheology is associated to the continuous phases homogeneously dispersed from lithium cobalt oxide and Super P, while adding more Super P induces lower shear rate due to higher viscosity resulted from the discontinuous phases of agglomerated Super P and voids under applied flow stress. Printing behavior enabling tunable line width relies on the synergistic interaction of ink rheology and processing condition, where controlling of the stretching region in thinning mode determines the printed line width under specific extrudate volume and moving speed. Furthermore, fluid dynamics combining with Darcy-Weisbach equation provides a model to predict the printing accuracy by calculating the flow rate and pressure drop, which exhibits a high consistency with experimental observations owing to the comprehensive consideration of various ink properties and printing parameters. This study provides systematic electrode printing guidelines for extrusion-based additive manufacturing, offering valuable insights for the development of high-performance lithium-ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D printed battery
Li-ion transport
Ink rheology
Printing behavior
Parametric modeling

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of geometry control for high-performance lithium-ion battery fabricated by extrusion-based additive manufacturing.

Electrochemical performance of cells with printed linear LCO cathode comparing to cell with tape-casted cathode: (a) voltage profile, (b) rate performance and (c) cycle performance. (d) The average specific capacities at C/10, 2C, 7C, and 20C with corresponding optical photos of cathodes, showing good reproducibility and data reliability. The error bars were calculated based on four specimens in each condition.

Changes of current and voltage with time during one charge/discharge cycle at (a) 0.1C and (b) 7C for cell with DIW-T50/W142 cathode. (c) Current drop with time during CV charge at 7C for cells with various cathodes. (d) Schematic diagrams of cathode utilization with different structures during charging process. (e) Rate capability, specific surface area, and areal mass loading of the printed linear cathode with different line widths. (f) Comparison of cells' energy density and power density with printed linear cathode and tape-cast cathode.

(a) Schematic diagram of DIW printing for three inks with different properties and corresponding photos of printed lines at the same printing condition (printing speed 5 mm/s, air pressure 0.4 MPa). Rheological properties of inks with different weight ratios of LiCoO2 to Super P: (b) viscosity as a function of shear rate, (c) storage modulus and loss modulus as a function of shear stress, and (d) shear stress as a function of shear rate, where solid lines are from fitting with Herschel-Bulkley model, and inset is the photo of ink III spilling out of the parallel plates. (e) The ink flow with large amount of Super P under the application of stress, where lower speed gradient would generated due to the agglomeration of large amount of Nano-sized Super P.

(a) Comparison of printable region for ink I, II, and III, where “Χ” indicates discontinues lines and the absence of valid data under the corresponding printing conditions. (b) L/N ratios of ink I, II, and III vary as a function of printing speed, indicating the influence of speed on the L/N ratio for each ink. (c) Schematic diagram of printing behavior under thinning mode of extrusion-based additive manufacturing [34], in which the upper-right picture is the digital photo of printed LCO lines.

(a) Contour mappings of the line width based on data from experimental results within the printable region of ink II using a 0.52 mm nozzle in Fig. 5(a). (b) Degree of similarity between calculated results using two methods and experimental data under different printing conditions. Contour mappings of the line width calculated from (c) direct calculation model and (d) corrected calculation model, providing insights into the consistency between experimental and calculated values and improved accuracy of corrected modeling.

Contour mappings of the line width printed with ink II using a 0.14 mm nozzle, based on data from (a) calculation results using corrected parametric modeling and (b) experimental results, verify the reliability of the proposed model. (c) Optical view of cathode with line width printed under the corresponding printing condition marked in (a). (d) 3D mapping of the printed linear cathode in 4 × 4 mm area. (e) Cross-section view and (f) porous microstructure of printed linear LCO cathode. (g) LCO patterns with line widths of 180 μm, 160 μm, and 140 μm printed under the printing conditions ①, ②, and ③, respectively.
The ink composition of ink I, II, III.
Calculated specific surface area and areal mass loading of LCO cathode with different structure.
Hershel-Bulkley parameters of ink I, II, III comparing yield stress achieved from storage modulus G’

head.ce:title: Printing guidelines for extrusion-based additive manufacturing with potential use in lithium-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of 3D Printing Materials, Korea Institute of Materials Science (KIMS), Changwon, 51508, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of 3D Printing Materials, Korea Institute of Materials Science (KIMS), Changwon, 51508, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Materials Engineering, University of Science & Technology (UST), Changwon, 51508, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Materials Engineering, University of Science & Technology (UST), Changwon, 51508, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of 3D Printing Materials, Korea Institute of Materials Science (KIMS), Changwon, 51508, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Extrusion-based additive manufacturing significantly affects the performance of lithium-ion battery through structural heterogeneities, while there are no printing guidelines able to predict accuracy of printed features. Herein, an empirical model for geometric prediction is proposed and verified to fabricate 3D battery based on fundamental understanding of ink rheology and printing behavior. Lower viscosity of ink rheology is associated to the continuous phases homogeneously dispersed from lithium cobalt oxide and Super P, while adding more Super P induces lower shear rate due to higher viscosity resulted from the discontinuous phases of agglomerated Super P and voids under applied flow stress. Printing behavior enabling tunable line width relies on the synergistic interaction of ink rheology and processing condition, where controlling of the stretching region in thinning mode determines the printed line width under specific extrudate volume and moving speed. Furthermore, fluid dynamics combining with Darcy-Weisbach equation provides a model to predict the printing accuracy by calculating the flow rate and pressure drop, which exhibits a high consistency with experimental observations owing to the comprehensive consideration of various ink properties and printing parameters. This study provides systematic electrode printing guidelines for extrusion-based additive manufacturing, offering valuable insights for the development of high-performance lithium-ion batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries, recognized as pivotal and extensively utilized electrical energy devices, have garnered significant attention owing to their high operating voltage, extended lifespan, and high energy storage density. They have been widely integrated into various energy sectors, including electronic devices, electric vehicles, and energy storage systems [1–3]. However, their performance and efficiency can be constrained by the sluggish diffusion of Li-ions within the monolithic electrodes. Fortunately, additive manufacturing, also known as 3D printing, offers an optimal solution by enabling the fabrication intricate structures that significantly enhance Li-ion utilization through improved Li-ion transport [4]. Direct ink writing (DIW) stands out as one of the most versatile 3D printing technologies, given its widespread availability for a diverse range of materials such as polymer, metal, ceramics, composite. DIW has been successfully employed in lithium-ion batteries research [5,6]. Nevertheless, the fabrication of structural electrodes using DIW in research often relies on a trial-and-error approach, posing challenges in ensuring reproducibility. In order to advance towards commercialization, there is a need for fundamental studies and systematic design strategies to establish reliable printing performance.

3D structural electrodes offer shorter diffusion pathways and reduced resistance for fast Li-ion transport, exhibiting prominent improvement of the electrochemical performance compared to conventional monolithic counterparts [7–9]. The larger surface areas of 3D structures can facilitate electrode reaction and ion transfer, leading to higher power density and higher energy density [10–12]. Researchers have extensively investigated structural electrodes fabricated by 3D printing, particularly DIW. Saleh, M. S. et, al [13]. fabricated 3D electrode architectures with micro lattice structure and hierarchical porosity, resulting in a 400 % increase in specific capacity, and a 100 % increase in areal capacity, and a high electrode volume utilization. Wang et, al [14]. studied the performance of LiFePO4 electrodes with three different geometric shapes, wherein an ultrathick 3D-patterned electrode exhibited a high areal capacity of 7.5 mAh g−1. Li et al. [15] prepared a hybrid 3D-structure LiMn2O4 cathode by DIW, demonstrating superior performances of 117.0 mAh g−1 and 4.5 mAh cm−2 in specific capacity and areal capacity, respectively. Despite the promising results, the exploration of the correlation between battery performance and electrode structure remains preliminary focused in the aforementioned studies. It remains crucial to develop a fundamental understanding of the entire fabrication process to achieve desired structural electrodes with high reproducibility, encompassing aspects ranging from ink design to printing behavior.

Prior to the printing of electrodes by DIW, ink design based on rheological behavior is necessary, involving viscoelasticity tests including viscosity and modulus behavior, which are crucial for precise control of geometry in printed architectures [9]. Achieving good printability of the ink requires appropriate viscosity and shear-thinning behavior to ensure continuous flow during the extrusion process. Meanwhile, stability of the green body typically depends on higher moduli to maintain its shape, although this may lead to susceptibility to clogging. Additionally, an increase of modulus resulting purely from the solid loading significantly raises the yield stress of ink, which has been observed to follow power functions of solid loading [16]. Hence, the ink design with anticipated printability is necessary to be investigated, providing sufficient stability of 3D structural electrodes. Moreover, printing parameters such as nozzle size, printing speed, and applied air pressure also play significant role in architecture prototyping [17]. However, the study on printing conditions are always overlooked by researchers and typically achieved through trial-and-error approach, which will result in significant time and material waste. Therefore, despite understanding the effects of ink rheology and printing conditions on the printability, quantitatively determining the correlation between these factors and printed line geometry is becoming a significant research trend as line geometry is an essential factor that directly influences the quality of final products fabricated through extrusion-based additive manufacturing [18]. An accurate, efficient, and time- and material-saving analytical method is needed to optimize the printing process and predict the printed line widths.

In this work, the Li-ion transport mechanism facilitated by structural cathode was investigated in terms of charging progress, in which the reasons resulting in faster Li-ion transport and improved performance in electrodes with narrower line width and higher specific surface were detailed discussed. To achieve the desired electrode geometry, a fundamental understanding of the effects of ink rheological properties and printing parameters on printing process was established first. Furthermore, a simple and accurate model was proposed for prediction of printed line width, comprehensively incorporating parameters related to ink rheological properties and printing conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 1 .

LiCoO2 (LCO, Nippon Chemical Industrial, Japan) served as active material, while Super P (MTI Korea Co., Korea) was utilized to enhance the conductivity of cathode. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, Solef® 6020, SOLVAY, Korea) was employed as a binder, dissolved in 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, ≥99.0 %, SIGMA-ALDRICH) at a weight percent of 6 wt%. LCO powders, Super P powders, and PVDF solution were mixed with a certain weight ratio for 10 min at 2000 rpm using a planetary mixer (ARE-310, Thinky, Japan) to achieve a homogenous ink. Three distinct inks, ranging from liquid-like to solid-like, were prepared by adjusting the amount of Super P, which not only influences the cathode conductivity but also affects the rheological properties due to its nanometer size [19]. As shown in Table 1 , the ink composition was adjusted by varying the weight ratio of LCO to Super P, while maintaining a total fraction of 96 wt%. The mass fraction of PVDF solution was consistently fixed at 4 wt%.

Direct ink witting (DIW, SHOT mini® 200SX, Musashi engineering, Japan) was selected due to its ease of use and cost-effectiveness. The ink was extruded from straight nozzle by pneumatic force, with the geometry size of the line controlled by adjusting air pressure, printing speed. Correlations between printed LCO lines and printing parameters were investigated under various printing conditions. Line widths under each condition were measured using a digital microscope (MSP-8000PRO, DIGIBIRD, Korea) after drying, and the average line widths and the ratios of line width to nozzle inner diameter (L/N) was calculated on a basis of 3∼5 lines.

The Ink II formulation was selected for the preparation of the LCO cathode. Prior to printing the linear cathode, 15 μm thick aluminum (Al) foil was punched into circles with a 14 mm diameter and affixed to a glass slide without any air, using ethanol. Excess ethanol was then removed with a clean wiper, and the Al foil was cleaned. Linear LCO cathodes with varying line widths (150 μm, 190 μm, and 230 μm) but similar line thickness (55 μm) were printed on the Al foil, which served as the current collector, compared with 55 μm laminated cathode fabricated by traditional tape casting method (Fig. S1). The electrodes were identified as “DIW-T55/Wxxx” or “TC-T55”, where “DIW-T55/Wxxx” represents the printed linear cathode by DIW with a line thickness of 55 μm and a line width of xxx μm. For instance, “DIW-T55/W150” indicates the printed linear cathode with lines of 55 μm thickness and 150 μm width, whereas “TC-T55” refers to the tape-casted cathode with 55 μm thickness. Printing parameters such as air pressure and printing speed were controlled to achieve the desired line width. Additionally, a comparative cathode was prepared by the traditional tape casting (TC) method with the same thickness as depicted in Fig. S1. Finally, the fabricated cathodes were dried at 80 °C to remove NMP, resulting in the cathodes composed solely of LCO, Super P, and PVDF. The line widths of printed cathodes were measured using a digital microscope after drying, and line thicknesses were measured with Al foil substrate after drying.

CR2032 coin cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box for electrochemical performance characterization. To ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the obtained data, four cells were assembled and tested for each kind of cathode. Li metal chips (MTI Korea Co., Korea) were used as the anode without any pre-treatment. A liquid electrolyte (PuriEL, Soulbrain Co., Ltd., Korea) with a composition of 1M LiPF6 in EC: DEC = 1: 1 + FEC 5 % + VC 1 % was used. Separator (Wellcos Co., Korea) was punched into a circular shape with a diameter of 16 mm before assembly. The galvanostatic charge-discharge tests of assembled coin cells were performed using an automatic battery cycler (WBCS 3000, WonAton Co., Ltd., Korea) within a voltage range of 3.0 V–4.2 V vs. Li/Li+. Continuous charges and discharges were performed after a 10 h rest at different current rates in an order of C/10, C/2, C, 2C, 5C, 7C, 10C, 20C, and 60C, during which three cycles were conducted at each current rate followed by cycle tests at C for 100 cycles.

Rheological properties of ink I, II, III were measured using a rheometer (MCR 92, Anton Paar, GmbH) with a 25 mm diameter parallel plate and a 1 mm gap at 25 °C. Sweep mode was applied to obtain viscosities of inks as a function of shear rate ranging from 0.01 s−1–200 s−1, and 50 points were sampled during testing. To determine the storage modulus (G’) and loss modulus (G”), oscillatory stress sweeps were conducted over a shear strain range of 0.01 %–100 % while applying a constant angular frequency ω of 10 1/s. 25 points were collected and plotted as a function of shear stress calculated from shear strain.

Morphologies of the printed LCO linear cathode, including top view, 3D mapping, cross-section view, and microstructure, were characterized by digital microscope (MSP-8000PRO, DIGIBIRD, Korea), stylus profilometer (DektakXT, Bruker, America), scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6610, JEOL, Japan) and field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JSM-7800F, JEOL, Japan), respectively. Specimens for 3D mapping test were prepared by attaching the printed linear cathode on a glass slide. A 4 × 4 mm area was scanned with an 80 μm trace step, applying 5 mg stylus force and 15 s duration time for every trace. For cross-section view, the dried cathode was cut with scissors and attached vertically to the sample holder with conductive tape. A protective copper foil was used to cover the surface to prevent falling off during measurement. The cross-sectional structure was detected with a 20-kV acceleration voltage in vacuum atmosphere. For the FESEM test, sample was attached horizontally without protective foil, and images were recorded with a 15-kV acceleration voltage a in vacuum atmosphere.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the voltage profile of discharge at different current rates during the second cycle. Both types of cathodes show the typical LCO discharge voltage profile, with the cell featuring the DIW-T55/W150 cathode achieving a maximum specific capacity of 151.1 mAh g−1. The specific capacity decreases with increasing current density, and the specific capacity of the cell with TC cathode declines more significantly than that with DIW printed cathode. For instance, the specific capacities at 10C are 117.1 mAh g−1 and 49.4 mAh g−1 for the DIW printed cathode and TC cathode, respectively, corresponding to reductions to 75.3 % and 34.9 % compared to their original values at C/10. In other words, 75.3 vol% of active materials were efficiently utilized at a relatively high current density of 10C for the DIW printed cathode compared to C/10, while only 34.9 vol% of active materials were utilized for TC cathode [20]. This discrepancy arises because the active particles of TC cathode near the current collector are not effectively utilized during lithiation/de-lithiation due to high tortuosity and suppressed Li-ion transport, which can be improved by introducing short and uniform ion diffusion pathways in the printed linear cathode [14].

The disparity in specific capacities between cells with different line widths of cathodes is illustrated in Fig. 2(b). The specific capacities of cells with printed cathodes remain almost identical below 20C, demonstrating the high stability and reproducibility of printed linear cathodes. Similarly, a progressive reduction in specific capacities occurs with increasing current density until 10C. Furthermore, significant differences in specific capacity are observed among cells with varying line widths at 20C. The specific capacity degrades with wider lines, obtaining 65.3 %, 51.4 %, and 42.0 % on a basis of initial specific capacity at 10/C for cathodes with 150 μm, 190 μm, and 230 μm lines, respectively. Additionally, the cell with TC cathode exhibits significant degradation in specific capacity starting from 7C and has a negligible rate capability of only 3.8 % at 20C, consistent with previous reports [15]. On the other hand, specific capacities recover to their original level upon reducing the current density to C, demonstrating good stability and reversible behavior.

The cycle performance of these cells was also evaluated, including specific capacities and Coulomb efficiency, based on a 100 cycles at C following the rate test, as shown in Fig. 2(c). All cathodes exhibit outstanding cycle performance with merely 3 % capacity degradation. The average specific capacities with deviations at several C rates were shown in Fig. 2(d), indicating good reproducibility and data reliability.

Changes in current and voltage over time during a charge/discharge cycle at various current densities were analyzed (Fig. 3(ãb) and Fig. S2(a)) to investigate the structural differences between linear and monolithic cathode in terms of Li-ion transport and cell performance. The second cycle of each target current density was chosen for analysis, with one cell selected for each cathode. Fig. 3(a) illustrates the typical configuration of cell current and cell voltage change during a full charge/discharge process. The charging process consists of two stages: constant current (CC) charge and constant voltage (CV) charge [21]. In the CC charge stage, the cell voltage increases while a constant current is applied until reaching the expected cut-off voltage of 4.2 V, after which the cell enters the CV charge stage. In the CV charge stage, cell is charged at constant cut-off voltage with a decreasing current until the current reaches the minimum threshold, which is C/20 in this work.

During the CC-CV charge process, the real potential difference between the cathode and anode is lower than cut-off voltage at the end of CC charge due to the voltage consumed by resistance, known as the polarization voltage. This polarization voltage is significantly influenced by the charge current. Effective Li-ion transport and nearly full-capacity charging are achieved at lower current, as illustrated in Fig. 3(a). Conversely, employing higher current induces elevated resistance voltage consumption and leads to incomplete charging capacity, demanding extended CV charging durations (as shown in Fig. 3(b)). However, notable differences in charging behavior were observed between cells with monolithic cathode and printed linear cathode at 7C. Apparent CC charging process and rapid current decline in CV charging were observed in linear cathode, while instantaneous CC charging and slow current decline exhibited by monolithic cathode (Fig. S2(a)). This phenomenon is attributed to enhanced effective Li-ion transport facilitated by the linear cathode and the introduction of channel, even at high current rates. As depicted in Fig. 3(d), Li-ions deintercalated from cathode and transport into electrolyte from all the surfaces of each line due to the presence of channels, whereas they can only access the limited surface area at the top for monolithic cathode.

Fig. 3(c) demonstrates the current drop during CV charge at 7C for cells with various cathodes, where currents were normalized for comparison. Cells with printed linear cathode exhibit similar and shorter CV charging time of 20 min, while the cell with TC-T55 cathode requires 36 min for entire CV charging process. Furthermore, as the currents of linear cathodes dropped to 3.5 % of the minimum threshold after 10 min, the cell with TC-T55 cathode only reached 52.4 %, showing a delay of 48.9 %, indicating fast Li-ion transport with the printed linear cathode.

To quantitively analyze the effects of cathode structure and line widths of linear cathodes on cell performance, the areal mass loading and specific surface area of each cathode were calculated. The areal mass loading of LCO was calculated using following equations,
(1)
P
L C O
=
m
L C O
m
L C O
+
m
S u p e r
P
+
m
P V D F
× 100 %
(2)
ρ A
=
m −
m
A l
f o i l
A
×
P
L C O
where P
LCO is weight percentage of LCO in dried ink. In this study, the weight ratio of ink II without NMP for cathode printing was LCO: Super P: PVDF = 87: 9: 4, resulting in a P
LCO value of 87 %. The areal mass loading of LCO (ρ
A ) represents the weight of LCO per unit area covered by lines. For the monolithic cathode, A is the area of Al foil. For the calculation of specific surface area (S
m ), the arc-shaped line was simplified to a rectangular solid, as shown in Fig. S2(b). S
m was calculated by equation (3),
(3)
S m
=
S
m
L C O
=
l ∙
(
2 T + W
)
m
L C O
where S is the total external surface area of cathode materials, and l is the total length of all lines. For the TC cathode, S is the area of Al foil. The calculated results are listed in Table 2 and plotted in Fig. 3(e).

The results demonstrate that the areal mass loading increases with wider line width. The maximum areal mass loading of DIW printed cathode is 3.47 mg/cm2 with the widest line which is more than two times smaller than 8.14 mg/cm2 for monolithic cathode. High areal mass loading is preferred to significantly enhance overall energy density, as reported [22]. However, despite having the highest mass loading, the monolithic cathodes didn't perform as expected, while cell with printed linear cathodes are more favorable for high-energy density and high-power density application (Fig. 3(f)). Additionally, the monolithic cathode exhibited only 3.1 % rate capability at 20C compared to C/10, whereas the lowest value for DIW printed cathode was 36.2 %, even with similar thickness. Furthermore, the rate capability of cells with printed linear cathode follows the same trend as specific surface area.

These phenomena can be explained by efficient reduction of tortuosity and improvement in Li-ion transport kinetics achieved by printed linear cathode with the introduction of channel, as discussed earlier. In contrast, high tortuosity and suppressed Li-ion transport occur in the bulk electrode at high current densities. Moreover, the degraded rate capability with wider line indicates that cathodes with narrower lines are more favorable for higher specific capacity at the same thickness due to higher specific surface area, resulting from the reduced total diffusion path. Consequently, enhanced intercalation and deintercalation of Li-ions into/out of the host electrodes through 3D architectures leads to an improved utilization of electrode volume and capacity.

In summary, the printed cathodes with narrower line width contributes to superior rate capability and overall performance of lithium-ion batteries. This emphasizes the importance of gaining a fundamental understanding of ink rheological properties and printing behavior to achieve the desired line sizes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ink rheology and printing behavior affecting regulable printing
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 (a), improper ink property can lead to collapse and discontinuity when the ink transitions from liquid-like to solid-like state, which was observed under the identical printing conditions (Printing speed: 2 mm/s, air pressure: 0.3 MPa). Liquid-like ink I is the easiest one to extrude, resulting in the widest as-printed lines. However, these lines subsequently collapse, becoming wider and thinner (blue area compared to the green as-printed line of ink I). In contrast, continuous line could not be printed thoroughly for solid-like ink III due to lower flowability, unless the printing speed is reduced to below 1 mm/s at the same air pressure. Compared to ink I and III, ink II possesses appropriate properties, exhibiting excellent printability with uniform and stable lines due to its compatibility with the printing conditions.

Rheological properties including viscosity and modulus of ink I, II, III were quantitatively analyzed, as they are essential for smooth printing [23]. The viscosity as a function of shear rate shown in Fig. 4(b) indicates a typical shear-thinning behavior, where the viscosities of all three inks decrease with increased shear rate, which is required for DIW printing. The shear rate of ink can be calculated based on the following equation [24],
(4)
γ ˙
=
4 Q
π
R 3
=
4 V π
R 2
π
R 3
=
4 V
R
where Q is the volume flow rate of ink, R is the radius of the nozzle (70 μm), and printing speed (2 mm/s) was applied as the flow velocity V. The shear rate during printing process is calculated as 114 S−1, corresponding to 2100 mPa s, 7710 mPa s, and 18790 mPa s for ink I, II, III, respectively, as indicated by the green line in Fig. 4(b). Ink I with lower viscosity is easier to flow out from the nozzle while ink III exhibits higher viscosity, resulting in less extrusion and discontinuous lines.

Fig. 4(c) presents the storage modulus and loss modulus as function of shear stress, demonstrating that all inks exhibit typical viscoelastic behavior. Before the crossing point of storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″), the inks display solid-like characteristics (G’ > G″). However, after this point, they exhibit liquid-like behavior (G’ < G”). It is worth noting that ink I exhibits a notably low storage modulus, rendering it unable to maintain its shape after printing, which aligns with observations in Fig. 4(a). Viscosities at specific shear rates (0.1 s−1, 1 s−1, 10 s−1, 100 s−1) and storage modulus at certain shear stresses (1 Pa, 10 Pa, 100 Pa) were also further explored as functions of the weight ratio of Super P and LCO, as shown in Fig. S3. Inks II and III did not have available storage modulus data at lower shear stresses due to the higher starting shear stress, which were estimated to be same in the LVR [25]. Apart from decreasing with increased shear rate, the viscosity of each ink also increased with the weight ratio of Super P, even when the weight ratio of major powder, LCO, decreased. A similar phenomenon was observed for the storage modulus, which can be attributed to increased solid loading [25,26]. As shown in Fig. S3 (c), comparing ink I, ink II, and ink III with a 5 wt% increase in Super P and an equivalent decrease in LCO, the corresponding volume varied by increments of 17.66 vol% and 11.26 vol% for Super P and decrements of 4.51 vol% and 2.87 vol% for LCO, respectively. Consequently, this led to an increased total solid loading of 40.59 vol%, 53.74 vol%, and 62.13 vol% for ink I, ink II, and ink III, respectively. The viscosity of suspension increases with the rise in solid loading, as predicted by the Krieger–Dougherty (K–D) equation, aligning well with experimental results [27].

The high shear stress observed for ink III indicates a higher air pressure requirement for ink extrusion during printing, which can be represented by its yield stress. Various methods have been reported for yield stress calculation [28]. In this work, we applied two commonly used methods: fitting the Herschel-Bulkley model and evaluating the viscoelastic properties. Firstly, the yield stress was calculated using the Herschel-Bulkley model [29], defined as:
(5)
σ =
σ y
+ κ
γ ˙
n
where σ represents the measured shear stress, σ
y is the yield stress of each ink, κ is the consistency index,
γ ˙
is the applied shear rate during testing, and n is the flow index. As depicted in Fig. 4(d) and Table 3 , the yield stress shows increasing trend with the weight ratio of Super P. Additionally, all inks exhibited shear-thinning behavior since the flow indices (n) were less than 1. The yield stress of ink III was determined by extrapolating the shear stress at the lowest
γ ˙
due to an abnormal shear stress-shear rate curve resulting from its ultra-high viscosity and ink spilling out of the parallel plates [28,30], as shown in the inset of Fig. 4(d).

Secondly, the yield stress of each ink was determined based on the viscoelastic property [31]. It corresponds to the shear stress at the end of linear viscoelastic region (LVR), where G′ starts to significantly decrease, indicating the onset of internal structure breakdown and irreversible deformation. The yield stress values obtained from this method present consistent trend with those from the first method. Notably, the yield stress of ink III was dramatically higher than that of the other two inks, suggesting the demand of much higher air pressure to ensure smooth extrusion from the nozzle, which may lead to nozzle clogging. Fig. 4(e) schematically depicts the effect of large amount of Super P nanoparticles on the ink's rheological properties. As reported previously, the increase of particle fraction would directly influence internal viscous shear stresses [32]. With the addition of more Super P, the dispersed nanoparticles in the fluid could consequently increase the internal friction between two layers of the ink when subjected to shear stress, leading to a lower velocity gradient, i.e., the viscosity of ink III is increased. In addition, van der Waals attraction is usually involved in the agglomeration of nanoparticles [33], which forms larger clusters and hinder the movement of other particles, thus producing a more viscous and stable ink.

Besides ink rheological properties, the geometry control of printed structure is also significantly influenced by printing conditions, including parameters such as nozzle size, air pressure, printing speed. To ensure precise geometry, printing resolution is a crucial factor primarily influenced by the size of the nozzle [6,8]. Since the focus of this study lies in the investigation of other printing parameters and their effects, the influence of nozzle size will not be further discussed, while a nozzle with an inner diameter of 0.52 mm was employed. To investigate the impact of printing conditions on line geometry, LCO lines were printed using ink I, II, III under various printing parameters (Fig. S4). The line width printed under various printing conditions were measured, and the ratios L/N to nozzle diameter were calculated.

As shown in Fig. 5 (a), although all three inks were printed using the same nozzle, higher air pressures were required for ink II and ink III due to their higher yield stresses. Notably, ink III exhibited more discontinues lines and a smaller printable region, even when 20 times higher air pressure was applied. In addition, all L/N ratios of ink I were greater than 1 across the entire printable region, indicating shape collapse due to its lower G’. In contrast, with proper ink rheological properties, ink II exhibited a reasonable L/N range from 0.5 to 1.9, suggesting better shape stability and printability. The changes in L/N ratios with varying printing speed and air pressure are demonstrated in Fig. 5(b), highlighting the relationship between process parameters and the resulting printed line geometry.

The printed line width becomes narrower with increasing printing speed while maintaining a constant air pressure. This phenomenon is consistent with the findings reported by Hyunwoo Yuk et al. [34]. At a given air pressure, the extrusion speed remains constant. Consequently, higher printing speed (nozzle moving speed) leads to greater stretching and a longer stretching region, resulting in a narrower line. Furthermore, higher air pressure results in a larger amount of ink being extruded compared to lower air pressure. This explains why the line widths printed at higher pressures are consistently wider than those printed at lower pressure for all three inks in the whole range of printing speed. Similarly, line width shows an increasing trend with increased air pressure at certain printing speed. As reported by Hyunwoo Yuk et al. [34], when printing speeds were faster than ink extrusion speed, the extruded ink undergoes stretching after swelling from the nozzle tip due to the motion of nozzle, which is schematically illustrated in Fig. 5(c). The upper-right picture of that shows digital photo of printed lines, where smooth line borders and uniform line widths indicate excellent printability and reliability of ink. In a word, line width is directly proportional to air pressure while being inversely proportional to printing speed, depending on the degree of stretching.

Ink II was selected as the focus due to the effective experimental data, attributed to its good shape stability and printability, which stem from its proper ink rheological properties. Fig. 6 (a) presents contour mapping of the line width for ink II within the printable region in Fig. 5(a), illustrating the distribution of line width under varying printing conditions. In the lower-right corner, less extrudate experiences large stretching degree from faster printing speed, resulting in narrower line width. On the contrary in the upper-left corner, excessive extrudate from high air pressure accumulated deposit on the substrate, leading to wider line than nozzle diameter.

Modeling of flow rate is typically the first step in geometry calculation for extrusion-based additive manufacturing. Since a straight nozzle was used in this work is, the flow rate Q can be calculated based on the mechanism of non-Newtonian fluid flowing through a cylindrical pipe [35]. It provides a foundation for predicting the final printed line geometry by linking flow dynamics with the printing process parameters:
(6)
Q =
π
R 3
1 n
+ 3
(
Δ PR
2 L k
)
1 n
where, n and k are flow index and consistency index from equation (5), R is pipe radius (0.26 mm), L is pipe length (13 mm), ΔP is pressure drop in the pipe. To simplify the modeling in calculation of line width, the arc-shaped line was simplified to a rectangular solid, as shown in Fig. S2(b). The line width is given by
(7) W = Q/TV
Where W is line width, Q is the flow rate, T is line thickness setting as same to the gap distance between nozzle tip and substrate (0.4 mm), V is printing speed.

Firstly, the direct calculation of line width under different printing parameters, using the applied air pressure as the pressure drop in equation (6), was performed and presented in Fig. 6(c). As shown, this approach resulted in a significantly different line width distribution and demonstrated a substantial deviation from the experimental results. This can be explained by the overestimation of pressure drop in the nozzle. A large pressure drop would be caused by the significant increase in flow velocity due to the reduction in pipe diameter from the syringe to the nozzle, effectively reducing the pressure drop within the nozzle [36,37].

To precisely predict the geometry of printed line, a simple and accurate model was proposed by correcting the pressure drop in the nozzle using the Darcy-Weisbach equation in fluid dynamics. For a power-law fluid under laminar flow condition, the pressure drop through a cylindrical pipe can be expressed by Refs. [38,39],
(8)
Δ P = f ∙
L
2 R
∙
ρ
v ‾
2
2
(9)
R e
G
=
ρ
v ‾
2 − n
(
2 R
)
n
k
(10)
f =
16
R e
G
where, ρ is the density of fluid, Re G is generalized Reynold number, f is the friction factor,
v ‾
is the average flow velocity. Combining equation (8)–(10), the formula for pressure drop can be expressed as:
(11)
Δ P =
8 k L
v ‾
n
(
2 R
)
n + 1

In terms of average flow velocity, it was confirmed by the weighing method due to the difficulty in in-situ measurement [37]. The mass m of extruded ink was measured for certain time (t = 90 s) under different air pressure, and then the average extrusion velocities were calculated by
(12)
v ‾
=
m
ρ t A
The average extrusion velocities under air pressure of 0.140 MPa, 0.145 MPa, 0.150 MPa were 0.620 mm/s, 1.038 mm/s, 1.165 mm/s, respectively.

Fig. 6(d) illustrates the corrected calculation results of line width based on equation (6)–(12). It is evident that the corrected calculation results exhibit an almost identical change distribution when compared to the experimental data, demonstrating reliability of the proposed model. However, the line widths calculated from the corrected model are still smaller than the experimental results. There are two possible reasons for this: ① overestimation of volume caused by simplification of printed line shape. A rectangular cross section occupies more space than one with arc surface, leading to a narrower calculated line width. ② underestimation of flow rate affected by wall slip, which effectively increase the flow rate but is not considered in this model, which has been thoroughly investigated and discussed by Kevin T. et al. [40]. These factors contribute to the observed discrepancies between the calculated and experimental line widths. Deviations of two calculation methods from experimental results is given in Fig. 6(b) in terms of ratio of calculated results to experimental results. All predictions are overestimation of line width printed under all printing conditions, as indicated by > 1 ratio values, which caused by overcalculation of applied pressure. On the contrast, all ratio values are <1 for the results from underestimation of corrected modeling as reasonably explained above. However, the deviation from corrected model have much lower ratio values (0.17–0.43) than that from direct calculation (12.19–28.78), indicating significantly improved prediction accuracy.

To verify the applicability of the proposed model, the line widths printed with a micronozzle (140 μm) and ink II were predicted and experimentally verified, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). This was particularly important due to the difficulty in accurately controlling ink flow rate at smaller size scales, where such precision is crucial for achieving desired print geometry. The prediction range was roughly determined by the extrusion conditions under various air pressures before the calculation. In the figure, only the data points that were verified by experiment are presented. The average extrusion velocities under air pressure of 0.35 MPa, 0.40 MPa, 0.45 MPa, and 0.50 MPa were measured by same experimental method, which are 0.802 mm/s, 0.955 mm/s, 1.298 mm/s, and 1.757 mm/s, respectively. Compared to the experimental results shown in Fig. 7(b) under the same printing conditions, the results from the corrected modeling exhibit an almost identical distribution, while a significant difference in the distribution of line widths from the direct calculation is shown in Fig. S5. This consistency with previous results ensures the accuracy of the predicted line widths within the given pressure range and confirms the reliability of the proposed model. However, a greater deviation in the values of the line width was observed compared to the corrected calculation for the larger nozzle. This suggests that the accuracy of the model decreases at smaller nozzle sizes, possibly due to increased slip effects on the overall flow rate that become more prominent at reduced scales. This occurs because slip velocity increases significantly with rising apparent wall stress in smaller nozzles [40].

A linear LCO cathode with a line width of 140 μm was printed under the predicted printing conditions, as marked in Fig. 7(a). Fig. 7(c) presents optical images of the top view of the printed LCO cathodes, clearly demonstrating straight line borders and uniform line widths. These features are further validated through 3D mapping, as depicted in Fig. 7(d), which exhibits consistent line thickness throughout the printed cathodes. To gain insights into the actual shape of the printed cathode lines, cross-section analysis was conducted SEM, as shown in Fig. 7(e). The SEM images confirm the consistent shape observed in the 3D mapping, reinforcing the accuracy and reliability of the characterization results. Furthermore, high-magnification SEM imaging (Fig. 7(f)) reveals the porous microstructure of the LCO cathode. The images show the presence of pores, fine Super P particles, and dense and smooth LCO secondary particles composed of smaller primary particles. The distribution of pores within the LCO particles is crucial as they will be filled with the liquid electrolyte, facilitating efficient ion transport during cell operation [20]. Additionally, the fine Super P particles homogeneously surround the LCO particles [41], serving to significantly enhance the electrical conductivity of the electrode. Furthermore, LCO patterns with line widths of 180 μm, 160 μm, and 140 μm were printed under the printing conditions ①, ②, and ③, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7(g), demonstrating excellent regulable printability of ink.

Linear LCO cathodes with different line widths but similar line thicknesses were fabricated using the DIW method and compared to monolithic cathodes produced by the traditional TC method. It was revealed that structural cathode with higher specific surface area exhibited faster Li-ion transport and improved performance owing to reduced tortuosity and enhanced Li-ion transport. To achieve smooth printing and precise geometry control of LCO cathodes, the effect of rheological properties of the ink and printing behavior were investigated fundamentally.

The ink with a composition of LCO: Super P: PVDF = 87: 9: 4 demonstrated optimal printability due to its appropriate rheological behavior, enabling smooth ink extrusion and printing. The agglomeration of nanosized Super P increased the internal friction between LCO and Super P nanoparticles, leading to reduced flowability. On the other hand, to precisely control the size of printed line, printing behavior and structure prototyping of the optimal ink were studied by adjusting printing parameters. A thinning mode for extrusion-based additive manufacturing was employed, where the stretching degree during printing identified as a crucial factor affecting the printed line width. Furthermore, a simple and accurate analytical model was proposed to quantitatively predict the printed line geometry based on ink properties and printing parameters. With this model, one can easily predict the printed line geometry by simply measure the extrusion velocity experimentally. This work offers guidelines for fabricating and developing high-performance electrodes for lithium-ion batteries using extrusion-based additive manufacturing techniques.

Although progress is being made in our understanding of printing guidelines for extrusion-based additive manufacturing, there are still limitations and many aspects that need further investigation. Firstly, this study focused on typical ink compositions used in Li-ion battery, while different ink compositions may be required for various applications. Therefore, similar studies should be conducted for different ink systems to verify the applicability of proposed model across a broader range of materials and conditions.

Secondly, it should be noted that the accuracy of flow rate plays a significant role in the precise prediction of printed geometry, and it is highly affected by slip effects, which was not considered in this study. Given the pronounced effect of wall slip on flow behavior, particularly at smaller scales, further corrections to both the flow rate and ink rheology are necessary for achieving high-precision predictions.

In addition, as a key factor for calculating the pressure drop in the nozzle, the average extrusion velocity was experimentally measured in this study, which could introduce errors compared to the actual conditions. The accuracy of the modeling could be further improved with the development of an in-situ measurement system for extrusion velocity.

Finally, if the issues mentioned above could be addressed, it will be possible to develop an advanced extrusion system that comprehensively integrates in-situ parameters monitoring system and data analysis system with high-accuracy models. This would enable real-time adjustment of parameters, leading to more reliable printing of desired geometries.

Yangyang Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Binghuan Gao: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sujin Park: Validation, Methodology. Seongwan Jang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Hyeonjin Son: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Chang-Jun Bae: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Research Program of Korea Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP; grant number: 20228520000080) funded by the Government of Korea (MOTIE; Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy).
